title: The coming heat wave! - Greater Kashmir
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Jammu and Kashmir
full_text:
Rising temperature in Jammu and Kashmir, with mercury hovering above normal, is clear indicator of hot summer ahead! This is likely to trigger erratic weather patterns, hit hydrological regimes, increase glacier recession and subsequently affect agriculture, horticulture and hydropower generation.
J&K’s summer capital Srinagar sizzled at 31.7 degrees Celsius, 7.6 notches above normal on May 9. The mercury touched the 30-degree mark for the first time this season before climbing past 31 degrees Celsius. Qazigund area in south Kashmir recorded 31 degrees Celsius, 7.8 degrees above normal. Famous tourist resort Pahalgam registered 25.2 degrees Celsius, 4.5 degrees above normal. World famous ski resort Gulmarg in north Kashmir recorded 23 degrees Celsius— 8.3 degrees above normal. Jammu region too is witnessing above normal temperatures.
Experts have forecast that emerging El Niño event expected by mid-2026 is likely to influence Kashmir’s weather, bringing below-normal precipitation, higher temperatures. The World Meteorological Organisation has indicated that El Niño conditions may weaken the southwest monsoon across large parts of India and its effect will also be felt in J&K.
El Niño and La Niña are opposite phases of the El Niño–Southern Oscillation, a large-scale ocean-atmosphere system that shapes global weather patterns. El Niño involves unusually warm sea surface temperatures in the equatorial Pacific, while La Niña is marked by cooler conditions.
Warmer temperatures trigger snow and glacial melt off leading to higher river discharge early in the season and reduced flows later in summer. The shift increases water stress and increases risks of flash floods and forest fires. The India Meteorological Department, in its forecast has projected rainfall at around 92 percent of the long period average. This raises concerns as El Niño is often linked with deficient rainfall and above-normal temperatures. Kashmir is likely to face mild water stress this year following a sharp decline in snow levels across the wider Hindu Kush Himalayan region. A new assessment by the International Centre for Integrated Mountain Development (ICIMD) found that snow persistence- the duration snow remains on the ground- fell 27.8% below normal between November 2025 and March 2026, the lowest level recorded in more than two decades. Kashmir, part of the Western Himalayas within the Indus basin, is already seeing the effects of declining snowpack, though not at severe levels so far this year.
This winter as previous ones was warmer and mostly dry in Kashmir. On February 21, Srinagar surpassed its all-time February maximum temperature record with 21 degrees Celsius. Snowfall has been confined to J&K’s mostly upper reaches during the 40-day harshest period known as Chillai Kalan. Sudden spike in temperature has led to earlier melting of snow on mountains increasing flow in water bodies.
J&K has witnessed four snowless Chillai Kalans over the last two decades. The spring heat wave of 2022 and recurrent above-normal summer-autumn-winter temperatures have propelled increasingly snow-deficient landscapes. This has put the region’s glaciers under stress and increased the recession rate. Scientific studies have clearly revealed how Kolhai, the largest glacier of Kashmir’s Jhelum Basin, is retreating rapidly due to a spurt in temperature triggered by global warming and extreme pollution. Thajiwas, Hoksar, Nehnar, Shishram, and glaciers around Harmukh are melting fast. J&K is a multi-hazard prone Himalayan region which is vulnerable to natural disasters especially earthquake, floods, landslides, windstorms and soil erosion. J&K ranks third among the Himalayan areas which are vulnerable to climate change and fall in the highest seismic zones. Anantnag, Kulgam, and Ganderbal districts in Kashmir while Kishtwar, Doda, Ramban, Reasi, and Udhampur districts in Jammu division have emerged in high vulnerability category, in the data of frequent cloud bursts, flash floods, landslides of the last 15 years in J&K.
J&K Government has constituted an expert committee for Hazard, Vulnerability and Risk Assessment (HVRA) for the Union Territory. The panel will assess multi-hazard risk including seismic, flood, landslide, GLOF, forest fires and demarcate hazard and zonation framework; prepare J&K HVRA atlas; prioritise sectoral and intervention strategies.
To deal with possible floods and heatwave conditions in the country, Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Amit Shah chaired crucial high-level meeting in New Delhi to comprehensively review the country’s preparedness. During the meeting the Home Minister assessed the readiness of Central Government agencies, review early warning systems, resource deployment and inter-agency coordination, to ensure zero loss of life and minimal loss to property. Long-term flood mitigation measures being taken across the country, including protection of critical infrastructure projects, river management initiatives and scientific advancements in forecasting were also reviewed.
J&K has been facing erratic weather patterns and increasing frequency of natural calamities. How can we forget devastating floods in September 2014 which caused extensive damage in Kashmir. There has also been rise in cloudbursts and flash floods. There are clear indicators of climate change in Kashmir. What we can do is just to minimise damage and prevent loss of lives during natural calamities mostly triggered by haphazard development and damage to environment.
Being an eco-fragile zone, there is a need to undertake climate change mitigation measures in J&K. We must work to reduce emission of greenhouse gases, undertake afforestation, prevent felling of trees, conserve water bodies and wetlands. We need to go for sustainable development to balance developmental with ecological sustainability. We must define carrying capacity of eco-fragile areas and tourist destinations to regulate footfall. We have to care and own nature as our survival depends on it!
Author is Executive Editor,
Greater Kashmir
